explanation_md: `std::invalid_argument` does not match `std::runtime_error` (they are siblings in the hierarchy, not parent-child), so the first catch is skipped. The second catch matches. After the catch block finishes, execution continues to `"end"`.

prompt_md: You have two catch clauses: `catch (const std::exception& e)` and `catch (const std::runtime_error& e)`. You want `runtime_error` to be handled specifically. Which order is correct?

options.c: Order does not matter; C++ always picks the most specific match
options.d: You cannot have both; the compiler rejects duplicate base-class catches

explanation_md: Catch clauses are tried in written order, top to bottom. If `std::exception` comes first, it will match `std::runtime_error` (which derives from it) and the specific handler is never reached. Always order from most-derived to most-base.
`f()` throws, so `"after f"` is never reached. The catch block runs and prints `e.what()` which is `"err"`. After the catch, execution continues to `"done"`.

prompt_md: What can you legally throw in C++?

options.b: Only objects of class type; primitive types like `int` are forbidden
options.c: Any copyable value: integers, strings, class objects, and so on

explanation_md: C++ allows throwing any copyable type, including plain `int`, `double`, `std::string`, or any class object. However, best practice is to throw objects derived from `std::exception` so callers can catch them with a single `catch (const std::exception&)` handler.
